description: Sago Palm (Cycas revoluta) and related cycad species are among the most hepatotoxic plants known, with a case fatality rate exceeding 50% even with treatment. Every part is toxic — but the seeds ("nuts") are the most concentrated source. Cycasin, the primary toxin, causes rapid, severe liver failure that is often fatal.
name: Sago Palm / Cycads
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: cycasin (methylazoxymethanol glycoside) in all parts; seeds contain the highest concentration
Mechanism: cycasin is metabolised to methylazoxymethanol (MAM), a powerful alkylating agent causing hepatocellular necrosis and DNA cross-linking
Symptoms (within 15 minutes to 3 hours): vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, lethargy; severe liver failure follows: jaundice, bleeding, neurological signs, coma
Seeds are the greatest risk — 1–2 seeds can kill a cat; all parts carry life-threatening toxicity
Any suspected ingestion is a critical veterinary emergency — survival depends on immediate, aggressive treatment
scientificName: Cycas revoluta, Cycas cirinalis, Zamia spp.

symptoms.name: Vomiting, drooling, anorexia, severe abdominal pain, increased thirst, and diarrhea (which may be bloody or appear as black, "tarry" feces)
symptoms.onset: Initial clinical signs typically appear rapidly, often within the first 4 hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), profound hepatic necrosis, and severe liver failure
symptoms.onset: Progresses over 48 to 72 hours following ingestion

symptoms.name: Bruising, blood clots, petechiae (pinpoint spots of bleeding), ecchymotic hemorrhages, and generalized abnormal bleeding
symptoms.onset: Delayed; develops secondary to liver failure within 48 to 72 hours

symptoms.name: Lethargy, extreme weakness, ataxia (loss of coordination), tremors, paralysis, seizures, coma, and eventually death
